brief_document.title: Implement consumer cursors and message replay
brief_document.purpose: Implements consumer cursor tracking and message replay for durable channels. Cursors track each consumer's read position per partition using haematite's cas operation, ensuring that checkpoint updates never regress. On restart, a consumer reads its persisted cursor and replays missed messages in sequence order. This is the mechanism that turns durable storage into recoverable consumption — without cursors and replay, persistence is write-only.
brief_document.task: Build consumer cursor persistence (cas-based checkpointing) and the message replay path (read-from-offset iteration). The cursor must support three checkpoint policies: per-message, per-batch, and explicit flush. Cursor checkpoint uses cas(consumer_id + partition, old_offset, new_offset) — a stale checkpoint must fail with CursorRegression, never silently overwrite. The replay path reads from a given offset to the partition head, returning messages in sequence order. An empty range (offset equals head) returns an empty iterator, not an error. Scope boundary: this brief handles cursor state and replay only. It does not handle dedup (DUR-004), conversation state (DUR-005), or crash recovery orchestration (DUR-006).

requirements.title: Define consumer cursor with partition-specific offset state
requirements.spec: ConsumerCursor struct SHALL track a consumer's read offset for a specific partition. It SHALL hold consumer_id (String), partition_key (String formatted as "{channel_id}:{partition_index}"), and current_offset (u64). ConsumerCursor SHALL be constructable from a fresh state (offset 0) or from a persisted offset read from haematite. ConsumerCursor SHALL implement Debug and Clone.
acceptance: ConsumerCursor has fields: consumer_id, partition_key, current_offset.
ConsumerCursor::new(consumer_id, partition_key) initializes with offset 0.
ConsumerCursor::from_persisted(consumer_id, partition_key, offset) initializes with the given offset.
ConsumerCursor implements Debug and Clone.

requirements.title: Implement CAS-based cursor checkpoint
requirements.spec: WHEN a consumer checkpoints its cursor, THE SYSTEM SHALL call DurableStore::cas(consumer_id + partition, old_offset, new_offset) where old_offset is the cursor's current stored value. IF cas fails (old_offset does not match stored value), THE SYSTEM SHALL return DurabilityError::CursorRegression with stored and attempted offsets. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT overwrite a cursor unconditionally — cas is the only checkpoint path. WHEN checkpoint succeeds, THE SYSTEM SHALL update the cursor's current_offset to new_offset.
acceptance: checkpoint() calls DurableStore::cas with the cursor's current_offset as old_value.
A successful checkpoint updates the cursor's current_offset field.
A cas failure returns DurabilityError::CursorRegression.
There is no unconditional write path for cursor updates — only cas.
The cas key is formatted as "{consumer_id}:{partition_key}".

requirements.title: Resume cursor from persisted offset on restart
requirements.spec: WHEN a consumer restarts, THE SYSTEM SHALL read the consumer's persisted cursor offset from haematite using DurableStore::read_from or a dedicated cursor-read operation. IF a persisted offset exists, THE SYSTEM SHALL construct a ConsumerCursor at that offset and resume processing from that point. IF no persisted offset exists, THE SYSTEM SHALL construct a cursor at offset 0 (beginning of the partition).
acceptance: A consumer with a persisted offset at position 42 restarts with cursor at 42.
A consumer with no persisted offset restarts with cursor at 0.
The resume path uses the same cas key format as the checkpoint path.
Resume does not modify the stored cursor — it only reads.

requirements.title: Support per-message, per-batch, and explicit-flush checkpoint policies
requirements.spec: CheckpointDriver SHALL implement the three checkpoint policies from DurabilityConfig. WHILE policy is PerMessage, THE SYSTEM SHALL checkpoint after every message processed. WHILE policy is PerBatch(n), THE SYSTEM SHALL checkpoint after every n messages processed. WHILE policy is ExplicitFlush, THE SYSTEM SHALL checkpoint only when explicitly requested by the consumer. The driver SHALL track messages-since-last-checkpoint to implement batch counting.
acceptance: PerMessage policy triggers checkpoint after processing 1 message.
PerBatch(10) policy triggers checkpoint after processing 10 messages, not before.
ExplicitFlush policy only checkpoints when flush() is called.
The checkpoint driver does not hold its own DurableStore — it calls through the cursor's checkpoint method.
Messages-since-last-checkpoint counter resets to 0 after each checkpoint.

requirements.title: Implement replay_from returning ordered message iterator
requirements.spec: replay_from(store, partition_key, offset) SHALL call DurableStore::read_from(partition_key, offset, limit) and return an ordered sequence of deserialized MessageEnvelopes. Messages SHALL be returned in sequence number order within the partition. The replay function SHALL handle pagination internally if the store returns partial results, fetching subsequent pages until the partition head is reached.
acceptance: replay_from returns messages in ascending sequence number order.
Messages are deserialized from StoredEntry bytes back to MessageEnvelope.
The partition_key passed to read_from matches DUR-002's "{channel_id}:{partition_index}" format.
Replay fetches all messages from offset to partition head, handling pagination.

requirements.title: Handle empty replay range gracefully
requirements.spec: WHEN replay_from is called with an offset equal to the partition head (no new messages), THE SYSTEM SHALL return an empty iterator. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT return an error for an empty range — this is a normal condition after a consumer has caught up.
acceptance: replay_from with offset == partition head returns an empty Vec (or iterator yielding zero items).
replay_from with offset == partition head does not return Err.
replay_from with offset == 0 on an empty partition returns an empty result.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement dedup logic or idempotency key checking — that is DUR-004.
SHALL NOT implement conversation state or event sourcing — that is DUR-005.
SHALL NOT implement crash recovery orchestration — that is DUR-006.
SHALL NOT modify the DurableStore trait or DurabilityConfig — those are DUR-001.
SHALL NOT implement unconditional cursor writes — cas is the only checkpoint mechanism.
verification: cargo check compiles cursor and replay modules with no errors.
grep -rn 'fn checkpoint' cursor.rs uses cas, not unconditional write.
Test: checkpoint with stale offset returns CursorRegression.
Test: replay_from with offset == head returns empty result, not error.

adrs.title: Haematite (prolly tree) as the storage backend, not a general-purpose database
adrs.decision: Haematite is the sole storage backend. Content-addressed prolly tree with WAL buffer gives us branchable state, structural merge, hash-based sync, and actor-per-shard write parallelism — all properties that directly benefit the bus (conversation state branching, multi-node sync, zero-contention writes). The rejected alternative is pluggable backends (splits testing surface, dilutes the branchable-state differentiator, and adds abstraction overhead for a benefit that doesn't exist yet).
adrs.decided_by: Bono + Frodo + Sir Patrick Stewart convergence

checklist.text: Consumer cursor persists offset via cas(consumer_id + partition, old_offset, new_offset) to haematite.
cas failure (old_offset does not match stored value) returns DurabilityError::CursorRegression, never overwrites.
On consumer restart, cursor is read from haematite and processing resumes from the stored offset.
Checkpoint policy supports per-message, per-batch, and explicit-flush modes configured in DurabilityConfig.
replay_from(partition, offset) returns an ordered iterator of messages from the given offset to the partition head.
Replay respects causal ordering within a partition: messages are returned in sequence number order.
Replay of an empty range (offset equals partition head) returns an empty iterator, not an error.

stories.text: As an application developer, I want to checkpoint my consumer cursor after processing a batch so that a crash only replays messages from the last checkpoint, not from the beginning.
As an application developer, I want to replay messages from a specific offset so that I can reprocess a range of messages without resetting my entire consumer state.
As an operator, I want to inspect a consumer's persisted cursor offset so that I can determine how far behind a consumer is relative to the partition head.

constraints.text: No file over 500 lines. If a module approaches this limit, extract into submodules.
No panics in production code. .unwrap() and .expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains ONLY pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. No logic, no type definitions, no function bodies.
All public types implement Debug. All error types implement std::error::Error via thiserror.
Every haematite append call must include an expected_seq for optimistic concurrency. No blind appends.
Consumer cursor checkpoint must use cas (compare-and-swap), never unconditional write. A stale checkpoint must fail, not silently regress.
Durable conversation replay must be idempotent: replaying the same event log twice produces identical state.
Ephemeral channels must not hold a reference to the DurableStore. The type system must prevent accidental persistence calls on ephemeral channels.
Dedup TTL expiry must never delete a receipt that is younger than the configured TTL, even under clock skew up to 1 second.
All durability operations must propagate haematite errors as DurabilityError variants, never swallow or log-and-continue.

resolved_context.intention:
When this cluster is complete, a developer can declare a channel as durable and get message persistence, replay from offset, and crash recovery without changing their publish/subscribe code. A developer building complex processing can choose a durable conversation and get exactly-once semantics that are emergent from resumable processing — not from delivery guarantees and not from application-level idempotency boilerplate.

The durability layer should feel like a channel property, not a separate system. Setting a channel to durable adds persistence; setting it to ephemeral bypasses haematite entirely with zero overhead. The two exactly-once strategies (lightweight dedup contract and heavyweight durable conversations) are complementary tools chosen per-channel, not competing approaches forced on all consumers.

The bus's relationship with haematite is direct and mechanical: append for writes, cas for cursors, read for replay. No ORM, no query builder, no abstraction that hides what haematite is doing. Developers who understand haematite's append/read/cas model understand the durability layer.

provenance.quote: When this cluster is complete, a developer can declare a channel as durable and get message persistence, replay from offset, and crash recovery without changing their publish/subscribe code. A develop
